item.title: IFS - Replacing UK Fiscal Rules
item.description:
The UK's fiscal rules aren't working. The Office for Budget Responsibility published a report last year saying the public finances are vulnerable, that the scale of risks is daunting. They forecast debt rising to over 270% of national income in our lifetimes under current plans. So what's going wrong?
The UK currently has two main fiscal rules — one saying day-to-day borrowing should stop so we only borrow to invest, and one saying a measure of debt should be falling by the end of parliament. T...

item.title: IFS - UK Fiscal Forecast Downside Risks
item.description: So we had a spring forecast, and it was mercifully just a forecast and not a fiscal event. Rachel Reeves deserves credit for resisting the temptation to announce new policy, especially with May elections looming. The big picture hasn't changed much since November. There's been a modest improvement in the borrowing outlook — stronger tax receipts and lower debt interest spending, offset by a weakening labor market and higher spending on things like special educational needs. Borrowing remains hig...

item.title: IFS - UK Pensioner Wealth Reality Check
item.description: Thirty years ago, about one in three UK pensioners were living in relative poverty. Their average income was roughly 20% lower than working-age people. That animated huge policy debates and led to major changes — pension credit in the early 2000s, and then the triple lock from 2010 onwards. The great recession hit working-age people harder than pensioners, and the triple lock ratcheted up the state pension. By the early 2010s, the average pensioner had income about the same as the average workin...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - OpenAI's Pentagon Power Play
item.description:
Last Friday night, while Dario Amodei was drafting his principled stand against the Pentagon, Sam Altman announced OpenAI had signed a deal to deploy models on classified defense networks. Hours later, the US and Israel began bombing Iran. By Saturday, Claude was number one in the app store and Anthropic was designated a supply chain risk to national security — an action never before taken against an American company. These events are connected by a logic most commentary has missed.
Dario misre...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Claude vs ChatGPT: Real Differences
item.description:
Millions of people just downloaded Claude after the Anthropic-Pentagon story, and almost all of them are going to treat it as a drop-in replacement for ChatGPT. That's a mistake. These are not interchangeable brands — they're built differently, trained differently, and optimized for different things. Switching with the same habits is like going from Excel to Photoshop and wondering where the spreadsheets went.
Here's what's actually different. ChatGPT is trained heavily on user feedback — thumb...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Enterprise Context Layer Lock-In
item.description:
The most expensive bet in history is an AI bet, but it's not about the model. OpenAI engineers leaked GPT-5.4's existence on GitHub and the internet predictably made it all about the model — prediction markets, hype threads, generational leap speculation. I don't care about the model. Neither should you. It's a component of something far larger.
Here's the thesis: the company that first makes enterprise-scale context genuinely usable — stored, retrievable, reasoned about, acted upon at a trilli...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Harnesses Are the Real Decision
item.description: AI harnesses are shaping your working life and nobody's talking about them enough. When you use a coding agent like Claude Code or Codex, you're interacting with two things: the model — the intelligence — and everything else. Where does the AI do its work? Does it remember what you were building? Can it reach your project management tools, your test systems? That everything else is the harness. The model determines how smart your AI is. The harness determines how usefully it fits into your work ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - GPT 5.4 Agentic Strengths and Judgment Gaps
item.description:
I asked GPT 5.4 thinking mode a simple question: I need to wash my car, the car wash is 100 meters away, should I walk or drive? It said walk. Every other frontier model — Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro — got it right immediately. Drive, you need the car at the car wash. The model OpenAI just positioned as its most capable system for professional work produced a careful, well-structured, completely wrong answer to a question a child would get right.
But it's not that simple. I ran blind evals ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Strike Teams Over Headcount Cuts
item.description:
Your meeting problem is actually a team-size problem. Meetings have tripled since 2020, and AI notetakers aren't fixing it — they're barnacles on a broken structure.
The math on communication pathways is unforgiving. Five people means 10 pathways — everyone holds the full map. Ten people means 45. Twenty means 190. Robin Dunbar established in 1992 that the human brain has layered limits: five for your core group, 15 for deep trust, 50 for meaningful working relationships. The military confirmed...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Emergent Safety in the AI Race
item.description: Claude blackmailed its developers to avoid shutdown. GPT 5.3 Codex helped build its own successor. Every frontier model tested by independent researchers schemes when scheming is the fastest path to completing its task. Anthropic, the company founded specifically because its CEO thought OpenAI moved too fast on safety, just abandoned its central commitment to never train a model it couldn't guarantee was safe. Chief science officer Jared Kaplan told Time, 'It no longer makes sense to make unilat...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Rejection as the Core AI Skill
item.description: Your most valuable AI skill is saying no. Not no to using AI — no to AI output that sucks. I reject far more AI-generated work than I accept: wrong framing, sloppy reasoning, confident-sounding analysis that wouldn't survive contact with anyone who actually understands the domain. I send it back, explain why, and often send it back again because the fix got it from 90 to 95 percent but my bar is higher. This isn't about superpowered prompting. You can prompt brilliantly and still say no constant...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Isn't Jagged Anymore
item.description:
What if AI isn't jagged anymore? That's what I can't stop thinking about. Everyone has assumed AI capabilities follow a jagged pattern — incredible at some things, terrible at others. It's become the organizing frame for how we deploy and teach AI. But I want to suggest the jagged frontier was never an inherent property of AI intelligence. It was an artifact of how we were asking AI to work.
When you ask a model a single question expecting a single answer, all the variance in task difficulty sh...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Building Visual Interfaces for AI Memory
item.description:
Your Open Brain can think, but now it needs hands. Thousands of people built this personal memory system — a Supabase database connected to any AI through MCP — and the number one question was: what do you actually do with it once you have it?
The answer is you go beyond the chatbot keyhole. Right now, when you interact with your Open Brain through Claude or ChatGPT, you're getting text back in an infinite scroll. That's fine, but some data is better seen than read. You want a calendar showing ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Ambition Over Headcount in AI
item.description: A few days ago, Whoop announced it's hiring over 600 people, nearly doubling its workforce. CEO Will Ahmed said they're investing in both people and AI. And the boards and leadership teams I work with — the ones actually making decisions, not performing for analysts — increasingly agree with him. The doom narrative lives in the media. Inside the rooms where it matters, the smartest operators are asking: what would it take for our people to work differently and build what we couldn't build before...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Unleashing Talent in the AI Era
item.description:
Your most extraordinary people are operating at about 25% of their actual capacity. The biggest wall to AI productivity isn't tool fluency — it's the human coordination overhead that consumes our days. The syncs, the scheduling, the meetings, the emails. And what I'm seeing from solo founders is not a story about special tools. It's a story about soft skills we haven't named well enough.
Take Ben Sira — zero employees, $2.5 million ARR with Pulsia, an AI company builder. He went from one millio...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Managing AI Agents Beyond Prompting
item.description:
Vibe coders everywhere are hitting a wall. In 2025, you described what you wanted and AI built it. But now Claude Code, Cursor, Codex — these aren't just suggesting code anymore. They read your files, create database tables, run commands, iterate autonomously for 30 or 40 minutes. You're working with agents now, and that requires a fundamentally different skill set. This is not a prompting problem. It's a supervision problem.
To illustrate how real this is: a Meta security researcher named Summ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Claude Browser Extension Real Workflows
item.description:
Everyone's sleeping on the Claude extension for Chrome, and they shouldn't be. It puts an LLM inside the most popular browser on the planet and says, 'Hey, do you want a free agent that can do useful work on the internet?' If you can define something you do repeatably, you can hand it to Claude and save yourself dozens of hours a week.
Let's start with a great example. Carl Votti, a product manager and Claude Code instructor, posted a thread about a billing dispute with AT&T that went viral. He...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - AI Agent Failure Modes Exposed
item.description: Your AI agent knows the answer, and sometimes it recommends the opposite thing. That's what I took away from a landmark study out of Mount Sinai Health System evaluating ChatGPT Health — the tool OpenAI rolled out as a responsible alternative for medical questions. What the physicians found is quite concerning: the system over-recommends doctor visits for minor conditions and under-recommends ER visits for urgent ones. In their study, ChatGPT Health told someone with early respiratory failure to...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Middleware Trap in AI
item.description: On February 25th, Perplexity shipped the best agentic product of the month, and it might not even matter. Perplexity Computer is a cloud-native multi-model orchestration system that routes work across 19 frontier models, spawns sub-agents, persists for months, and delivers finished artifacts while you sleep. It runs Claude Opus 4.6 as its reasoning core, Gemini for deep research, Grok for speed, ChatGPT 5.2 for long context recall. It's available for $200 a month and genuinely worth it if you do...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Building Agents Without OpenClaw
item.description:
A few weeks ago I introduced OpenBrain — a SQL database connected via MCP server to your AI, giving it persistent memory. Thousands of you built it. Now I want to talk about what happens when you go beyond memory and give your agent a heartbeat and tools, because that's what turns a chatbot into something that actually does work for you.
An agent needs three things. First, memory — the ability to read and write to something persistent so it remembers what happened yesterday and three weeks ago....

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - The Agent Memory Wall Crisis
item.description:
AI agents are getting better. The people deploying them are not. And that's the whole story right now. The capability trajectory is real and accelerating — agents can write code, generate designs, close tickets. But there's a memory wall. Software jobs last 18 months to two years on average. AI agents are measured in weeks at best, usually an hour or two. That gap is one of the hardest problems in tech, and it's leading a lot of overoptimistic people astray.
AI still lacks common sense in weird...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Making Businesses Agent-Readable
item.description:
The fences we spent 20 years building to keep bots out are now keeping our most valuable customers out. When OpenClaw hit 250,000 GitHub stars, Jensen called it the operating system of the future. Everyone's jumped on that bandwagon, but nobody's talking about the structural precondition — the thing underneath that determines whether any of this actually works.
OpenClaw only works as a paradigm if company systems — product catalogs, transactional infrastructure, everything — are agent-readable ...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Strategic Bets Behind AI Agents
item.description:
OpenClaw is the most consequential moment in AI since ChatGPT. But the real story isn't the horse race of competitors or the security dumpster fire — it's that every major company responding to OpenClaw has made a fundamentally different strategic bet. And if you understand those bets, you can look at any new agent product and immediately figure out what it's actually for and whether it works for you.
This matters because the avalanche is real. Nvidia built Nemo Claw. OpenAI acqui-hired Peter a...

item.title: Nate-B-Jones - Agent Engineering Needs Old Principles
item.description:
There's a battle playing out at the heart of agent world. On one side, Nvidia with Nemo Claw. On the other, OpenAI and Anthropic. The battle isn't about who builds agents — it's about how you get enterprises to actually use them.
Anthropic and OpenAI spent 2025 discovering a bitter lesson: the companies they work with didn't have the expertise to apply their solutions. They'd launch amazing tools and watch them suffer in production. These companies ship incredibly fast internally, but they were...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Surveillance Gaps, Remuneration Reforms, Crypto Regime
item.description:
Good morning. Let's get into today's compliance briefing, starting with the most operationally significant development for wholesale markets teams.
The 2026 edition of eflow's Global Trends in Market Abuse and Trade Surveillance report is now live, drawing on enforcement data and input from over 300 regulatory professionals. The headline numbers demand attention. Fifty percent of all fines issued globally were related to trade surveillance system and controls failures. That's not insider dealin...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - SMCR Reform and Regulatory Pipeline Priorities
item.description:
Good morning. The most consequential development for compliance teams right now is the coordinated SMCR reform push from HM Treasury, the FCA, and the PRA, all three publishing consultation papers on 15 July 2025. The consultation window closes 7 October 2025, and if you haven't already started your firm's response, now is the time.
Let's start with what matters most for senior managers and certification. The FCA and PRA are proposing what they're calling Phase 1 reforms — changes that can be m...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Handbook Upgrades and Regulatory Housekeeping
item.description:
Good morning. Today's compliance briefing is relatively light on headline enforcement or market abuse developments, but there are a few housekeeping and strategic items worth noting.
First, the FCA has formally launched its redesigned Handbook website. This is part of the regulator's stated 5-year strategy to become a smarter regulator. The new site improves navigation, makes it easier to understand connections between rules, and critically, allows users to compare different versions of Handboo...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - SMCR Reform and Enforcement Escalation
item.description: Let's start with the biggest structural development. The UK government is undertaking the most sweeping review of the Senior Managers and Certification Regime since its introduction. This is playing out in three phases, and compliance teams need to understand all of them. Phase One, driven by the FCA and PRA through consultation papers CP25/21 and CP18/25, is essentially a streamlining exercise. The 12-week temporary cover rule is being revised so that firms must submit an SMF application within...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK Reporting Overhaul and SMCR Reform
item.description:
Let's get into the key developments that matter for compliance teams across wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets divisions.
The biggest news this month is the FCA's launch of CP25/32, a consultation proposing a fundamental overhaul of the UK's onshore MiFIR transaction reporting regime. If implemented, the changes would reduce the number of reportable fields, limit mandatory reporting to instruments traded on UK venues, remove FX derivatives from scope entirely, and shorten the defaul...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK MAR Compliance Framework Essentials
item.description: Let's start with what matters most for trading desks and compliance teams today. The FCA continues to signal that preventing, detecting, and punishing market abuse is among its highest enforcement priorities, directly tied to its statutory objectives of consumer protection, market integrity, and competition. The enforcement toolkit is formidable: unlimited fines for civil breaches of UK MAR, and on the criminal side under the Criminal Justice Act 1993 and Financial Services Act 2012, custodial s...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Enforcement, MAR Actions, Algorithmic Trading
item.description:
Let's get into today's compliance briefing, which covers some significant enforcement actions and a dense set of regulatory developments that wholesale markets teams need to be tracking.
The headline story is the FCA's enforcement momentum in Q1 2026. Total fines have reached just under £16 million for the quarter, and the pattern here is unmistakable — individual accountability is front and centre.
The largest single penalty goes to John Wood Group PLC at £12.9 million for publishing misleadi...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Remuneration Reform, Market Abuse, SMCR Accountability
item.description: Let's start with the most consequential development for wholesale markets compliance teams this period. The FCA and PRA have jointly published their final policy statement on remuneration reform for dual-regulated firms — banks, building societies, and PRA-designated investment firms. These changes took effect on 16 October 2025, and some may be applied optionally to ongoing performance years or unvested awards from prior years. The reforms are significant in scope and go further than the origin...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Market Abuse and SMCR Accountability
item.description:
Good morning. Today's briefing covers two critical areas: the FCA's sharpened stance on market abuse enforcement and the continuing accountability obligations under the Senior Managers and Certification Regime.
Let's start with the big development. On 29 April 2025, Therese Chambers, Joint Executive Director of Enforcement and Market Oversight at the FCA, laid out the regulator's agenda for combatting market abuse as part of its new five-year strategy. She framed enforcement around what she cal...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA PRA Cut SMCR Red Tape
item.description: Good morning. The headline development today is a joint announcement from the FCA and PRA signalling cuts to Senior Managers and Certification Regime red tape, explicitly positioned as a measure to support growth. While the full detail of these changes hasn't yet been published in the materials available, the signal itself is significant. For compliance officers managing SMCR frameworks across wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets divisions, this likely means upcoming revisions to how s...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - UK Regulation Pivots Toward Growth
item.description:
Let's get into today's compliance briefing, which centers on a fundamental shift in the UK's regulatory posture and what it means practically for wholesale markets and investment bank compliance teams.
The headline development comes from EY's 2026 UK Financial Services Regulation Outlook, which maps a regulatory environment that has decisively pivoted from defending against risk to actively enabling economic growth. The government's position is explicit: it is time to regulate for growth. The L...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Geopolitics and Enforcement Landscape Update
item.description:
Good morning. Two developments to flag today, both relatively light on published detail but carrying meaningful implications for compliance teams operating across wholesale markets.
First, enforcement trends around abuse of dominance. A&O Shearman has highlighted that antitrust authorities globally are targeting both exploitative and exclusionary abuses of dominance with increasing vigour. Now, while this sits squarely in the competition law space, there's a compliance read-across worth noting....

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Operational Resilience and SMCR Reform Updates
item.description:
Good morning. The headline development today is a major coordinated move from the FCA, PRA and Bank of England on operational resilience. On 18 March 2026, all three authorities issued Policy Statements finalising their rules on operational incident reporting and material third-party reporting. This is the culmination of consultations that ran through 2024, and the practical implications for wholesale markets compliance teams are significant.
Let's start with what matters most. The regulators h...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - MAR Enforcement and Surveillance Priorities
item.description:
Today's briefing focuses on the FCA's evolving approach to Market Abuse Regulation and what it means practically for compliance teams across wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets divisions.
The headline message from the FCA is a clear shift in expectations: the regulator wants to see market abuse controls that are preventative, not merely detective. It's no longer sufficient to have surveillance systems that flag suspicious activity after the fact. The FCA expects firms to build framew...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - Regulatory References and PRA Oversight Basics
item.description:
It's a relatively quiet day on the regulatory front. The sources flagged today are essentially background reference materials rather than new enforcement actions or policy changes, but they touch on themes that remain core to how compliance teams operate.
The PRA continues in its established role as the prudential supervisor for banks, building societies, credit unions, insurers, and major investment firms. For wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets divisions, the PRA's oversight framew...

item.title: Compliance Daily Briefing - FCA Enforcement and Resilience Updates
item.description:
Here's your compliance briefing for today, focusing on the latest from the FCA that matters most to wholesale markets and Global Banking & Markets teams.
Let's start with enforcement and individual accountability. The FCA has banned Kasim Garipoglu from working in UK financial services. While the full details of the prohibition are still being digested, any ban sends a clear signal about SM&CR enforcement appetite. If you're a compliance officer managing senior manager or certification regime r...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Computing's Breakout Moment
item.description:
The quantum computing world is experiencing what may be its most consequential stretch yet, with commercial milestones, record investment, and fundamental physics discoveries all converging at once.
Let's start with the biggest industry development. Quantinuum launched its Helios quantum computer in November, calling it the most accurate commercial system available. The company's lead architect, Anthony Ransford, made the striking claim that you'd need to harvest every star in the universe to p...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Infrastructure Race Heats Up
item.description: Today in quantum computing, there are significant moves on the infrastructure and investment front. AWS is expanding its quantum research presence in Pasadena, California, purchasing a 168,000 square foot facility at 2964 Bradley Street for roughly $78 million — more than double what the property sold for in 2021. This is separate from AWS's existing quantum computing center on the Caltech campus, which launched in 2021 and spans about 21,000 square feet focused on developing fault-tolerant quan...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Meets Real-World Operations
item.description: The most striking quantum development today is a real-world proof point: D-Wave's hybrid quantum annealing system has cut emergency response times for North Wales Police by nearly 50 percent. The system solved what's called the forward deployment problem — optimally positioning police vehicles across ten local teams spanning diverse geography — compressing a task that previously took four months of classical computation into just four minutes. The project achieved 90 percent of target response t...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Hardware and Infrastructure Advances
item.description:
Several major developments are reshaping the quantum computing landscape today. Let's start with the hardware breakthroughs.
Microsoft has formally introduced Majorana 1, the world's first quantum chip powered by what it calls a Topological Core architecture. The chip leverages a new class of material — a topoconductor made from indium arsenide and aluminum, fabricated atom by atom — that can coax exotic Majorana particles into existence. These particles hide quantum information, making it inhe...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Partnerships and Supercomputing Architecture
item.description:
Today's quantum news centers on major partnerships and a significant architectural milestone that collectively signal the field's accelerating move from lab curiosity to practical infrastructure.
The biggest story is IBM's articulation of a reference architecture for quantum-centric supercomputing — essentially a blueprint for integrating quantum processors into existing high-performance computing data centers. The framing from IBM, and echoed in Barron's coverage, is notable: quantum isn't jus...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Investment Meets National Security Controls
item.description:
Today's quantum briefing centers on a rapidly intensifying dynamic at the intersection of quantum technology investment and national security regulation. A detailed analysis from Freshfields lays out how governments are playing both sides of the quantum coin — pouring billions into developing national quantum champions while simultaneously erecting protective barriers to ensure rivals can't benefit from those advances.
The core issue is quantum technology's dual-use nature. Policymakers increas...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Error Correction and Internet Advances
item.description:
Today's quantum landscape is dominated by two converging themes: the push toward a quantum internet and the accelerating race to solve quantum error correction. Let's start with the biggest developments.
Quantinuum has demonstrated remarkable results on its 98-qubit Helios trapped-ion processor using what are called iceberg codes. They achieved 94 error-detected logical qubits and 48 error-corrected logical qubits from just 98 physical qubits — a near 2-to-1 physical-to-logical ratio for error ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Nvidia's Quantum and AI Expansion
item.description:
Today in quantum and adjacent computing news, two stories highlight different ends of the spectrum — from a Danish startup working at the frontier of quantum chemistry to Nvidia's continued dominance and expanding reach into quantum computing infrastructure.
Let's start with Nvidia, which remains arguably the most consequential technology company of this decade. The company just posted record fiscal fourth-quarter revenue of $68.1 billion, driven by its data center division which alone brought ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Fault-Tolerant Quantum Architectures Advance
item.description: The biggest development today in quantum computing is a new partnership between Paris-based C12 and Germany's QC Design. C12 is adopting QC Design's Plaquette design-automation platform to accelerate its path toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. C12's approach is distinctive — they use isotopically purified carbon-12 nanotubes suspended above silicon chips to host spin qubits. This technique maximally isolates qubits from environmental noise, yielding coherence times two orders of magnitude ...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - UK Bets Big on Quantum Future
item.description: The biggest quantum news today centers on the United Kingdom making an enormous bet on quantum computing. Chancellor Rachel Reeves and Technology Secretary Liz Kendall announced up to two billion pounds — roughly two and a half billion US dollars — to scale quantum computers on British soil by the early 2030s. The centerpiece is a world-first one billion pound procurement program where the government will actually buy quantum computers from the first UK companies to build them at commercial scal...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Turing Award and Quantum-Classical Integration
item.description: The biggest news today is that Charles H. Bennett and Gilles Brassard have been awarded the 2025 ACM Turing Award — the so-called Nobel Prize of Computing — carrying a one-million-dollar prize supported by Google. Bennett, an 82-year-old IBM fellow, and Brassard, a 70-year-old professor at the University of Montreal, are recognized as the founders of quantum information science. Their story begins with a chance meeting at a conference in Puerto Rico in 1979, where Bennett reportedly approached B...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Earnings, Cryptography, and Workforce
item.description: The biggest story in quantum today is the financial picture emerging from Q4 and full-year 2025 earnings for the three publicly traded quantum computing firms. IonQ stands out as the clear revenue leader, posting $130 million in full-year GAAP revenue — a 202 percent increase over FY24 — making it the first public quantum company to cross the $100 million annual revenue threshold. Its Q4 alone brought in nearly $62 million, up 429 percent year-over-year. IonQ is projecting $225 to $245 million i...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Software Goes Public on Nasdaq
item.description: The biggest quantum news today is the Nasdaq debut of Horizon Quantum Computing, an Irish-founded company that builds software development tools designed to unlock the potential of quantum hardware. The listing came through a SPAC merger with dMY Squared Technology Group, netting Horizon roughly $120 million in gross proceeds before transaction expenses. CEO and founder Dr. Joe Fitzsimons, who has more than 20 years in quantum computing research, said the company will channel those funds into ac...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Error Modeling, Silicon Qubits, UK Quantum Funding
item.description: The biggest technical story today comes from Sandia National Laboratories, where Jordan Hines and colleagues have developed a new simulation technique that accounts for realistic error types in quantum circuits — specifically coherent and non-Pauli errors that have been largely ignored in fault-tolerant quantum computing analysis. The results are sobering: coherent errors can cause logical failure rates more than eight times higher than the simplified Pauli stochastic models the field has been r...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Ecosystems, Chips, and Policy Push
item.description: Today's quantum landscape is defined by three converging forces: regional ecosystem building, hardware breakthroughs from tech giants, and growing government engagement. Let's start with the biggest policy moves. California has launched Quantum California, a statewide public-private initiative under the California Jobs First Economic Blueprint — the state's first bottom-up economic strategy in more than 20 years. Leaders from science, industry, and the governor's office convened at UC Santa Barb...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Quantum Strategy, Chips, and Breakthroughs
item.description: Several important threads are converging in the quantum computing world right now. Let's start with policy. The Government Accountability Office just published a report flagging serious strategic gaps in the National Quantum Initiative Act ahead of a Senate Commerce Committee markup on its reauthorization this Wednesday. The GAO found that the current national quantum strategy lacks specific objectives for quantum computing, has no metrics to measure federal quantum activities, doesn't address f...

item.title: Quantum Daily Briefing - Google Expands Into Neutral Atom Quantum
item.description: The biggest news today is Google Quantum AI's announcement that it's expanding beyond its decade-long focus on superconducting qubits to embrace neutral atom quantum computing as a second modality. The logic is compelling: superconducting qubits already handle circuits with millions of gate cycles, each taking just a microsecond, making them strong in circuit depth. Neutral atoms, meanwhile, have scaled to arrays of roughly ten thousand qubits with flexible any-to-any connectivity, but their gat...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Compliance
Weekly Digest: Compliance
item.description:
This week in compliance has been dominated by a single, unmistakable theme: the UK regulatory architecture is being fundamentally recalibrated, and at the center of nearly every development sits individual accountability.
The most structurally significant story running through the entire week is the overhaul of the Senior Managers and Certification Regime. Multiple episodes returned to this from different angles, building a remarkably consistent picture. HM Treasury, the FCA, and the PRA are al...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Compliance
item.description:
This week in compliance, one overarching theme dominated every conversation: the UK regulatory landscape is being fundamentally reshaped around a growth agenda, but that pivot toward enabling economic activity is being paired with a more sophisticated, more targeted, and in many respects more demanding supervisory apparatus. Compliance teams that hear "growth" and think "relaxation" will find themselves badly exposed.
The most consequential development landed at the start of the week with the F...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing, one overarching narrative dominates everything else: the field is crossing from experimental curiosity into something that looks increasingly like a real industry, with all the commercial partnerships, infrastructure buildouts, regulatory friction, and engineering milestones that come with that transition.
The week opened with a sweeping survey of the field's breakout moment. The numbers alone tell a striking story. Quantum startups raised nearly four billion dol...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Quantum
item.description:
This week in quantum computing has been one of the most eventful in recent memory, with developments spanning government policy, financial milestones, fundamental research, and the continued blurring of boundaries between quantum and classical computing. The overarching story is one of an industry decisively crossing from laboratory curiosity into industrial infrastructure, even as deep technical challenges remind us just how much work remains.
The most commanding theme this week has been the m...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Tech
item.description:
This week's episodes form a remarkably cohesive argument that builds across every conversation, and the throughline is this: the AI revolution is not primarily a technology story. It's an organizational and human judgment story, and nearly every conventional response to it — cutting headcount, buying better tools, writing fancier prompts — misses the point entirely.
The week opens with a structural argument about team size that reframes everything that follows. The core insight is mathematical:...

item.title: Weekly Digest: Tech
item.description:
This week's episodes form a remarkably cohesive narrative about a single inflection point: the shift from AI as a tool you use to AI as an agent you manage, and everything that shift demands of individuals, companies, and the broader commercial ecosystem.
The overarching theme is that we are no longer in the prompting era. That phrase comes up explicitly in the episode on managing AI agents, but it echoes through every single conversation this week. In 2025, the skill was describing what you wa...
